Name: Is there a distinct category of dual for verbal agreement with a dual subject?
Is non-agreement in number between the nominal subject and verbal predicate possible?
Can finite verbs agree in number with a nominal object?
Can an adnominal property word agree with the noun in case?
Is there non-agreement between an adnominal property word and the head noun?
Comment: In attributive clauses the modifier has no case/number marker.

Name: Can adpositions be used to mark core verbal arguments?
Can non-finite clauses use a special means for core argument (S/A) marking?
Can non-finite clauses use a special means for core argument (P) marking?
Comment: Cf. UT010.

Name: Do the characteristics of the object referent (topicality, animacy, definiteness) influence object marking?
Comment: Indefinite objects are in the nominative case, whereas definite objects are typically in the genitive case.

Name: Does object marking correlate with aspectuality?
Comment: Transitive verbs can agree with the object in person and number. Verbs in the objective conjugation express a bounded action, whereas verbs in the subjective conjugation (with definite objects) express unbounded actions. Furthermore, a definite object can also be in the inessive (the verb is in the subjective conjugation in this case) and this construction also expresses an unbounded action.

Name: Does object marking depend on the verb conjugation form?
Is there differential subject marking?
Can S and A be conflated morphologically across clause boundaries, i.e., act as a syntactic pivot?
Can S and P be conflated morphologically across clause boundaries, i.e., act as a syntactic pivot?
Is there grammatical marking of habituality?
Comment: PST2 expresses past habituality.

Name: Is there grammatical marking of frequentativity?
Is there grammatical marking of progressivity?
Comment: In addition to expressing repeated events, PST2 can also express events that were continuing in the past.

Name: Is it common to use a verb in the present tense for future time reference?
Comment: The forms of present cover both present and future time.

Name: Is there grammatical marking of the immediate future?
Is there grammatical marking of the pluperfect?
Can aspect be expressed overtly in the negative marker?
Comment: Aspectual oppositions can be expressed with the objective vs. subjective conjugation. In the past tense, the negative marker takes person marking, and the two conjugation types are differentiated in the negative marker.

Name: Can tense be expressed overtly in the negative marker?
Is there grammatical marking of mirativity?
Is there morphological marking on the verb dedicated to the expression of a desire or wish?
Is there morphological marking on the verb dedicated to irrealis?
Are verbs marked with a non-indicative mood in counterfactual conditional clauses?
Is there morphological marking on the verb dedicated to the expression of deontic necessity?
Comment: The infinitival participle -ma can mark necessity. The subject has to be in the dative case in this construction.

Name: Is there morphological marking on the verb dedicated to the expression of epistemic possibility?
Is there morphological marking on the verb dedicated to the expression of deontic possibility?
Source: Niina Aasmäe (p.c. 2020); Mariann Bernhardt and Denys Teptiuk (p.c. 2022)
Comment: The passive/reflexive marker v is used to express capability, possibility, passive voice (depending on a context).

Name: Is there morphological marking on the verb used to direct commands/wishes, etc. to the 3Sg?
Is there morphological marking on the verb used to direct commands/wishes, etc. to the 1Sg?
Is it common to use the imperative/jussive in complement clauses introduced by predicates expressing wish/desire/will?
Source: Niina Aasmäe (p.c. 2020); Mariann Bernhardt and Denys Teptiuk (p.c. 2022)

Name: Is there morphological marking on the verb dedicated to the expression of apprehension?
Is there a reflexive conjugation?
Comment: There is no reflexive conjugation, there is only the passive/reflexive marker v, e.g. Mar'a-n' tomba-v-s' ked'-eze (Mary-GEN hurt-PASS-PST1.3SG hand-POSS.3SG) 'Mary's hand got hurt'.

Name: Can non-singular 2nd and 3rd person have the same person/number marking or morphosyntactic treatment?
Comment: 2PL and 3PL subject are syncretic in the objective conjugation.

Name: Can the 3rd person plural verb form and impersonal (passive) verb form be encoded in the same way?
Are 2nd person singular verb forms commonly used with a generic meaning?
Can an infinitival verb form be used in a directive function?
Can verbs in purposive subordinate clauses be infinitive forms?
Comment: It is also possible to use Russian loanword štoba 'in order to' + infinitive.

Name: Are there sentences without overt verbal arguments?
Is a copula commonly used for predicate nominals in the 3rd person singular forms of the present tense?
Comment: Sonze kudo-zo mazij (3SG.GEN house-POSS.3SG beautiful) 'His/her house is beautiful'.

Name: Is a copula commonly used for predicate nominals in the past tense?
Source: Niina Aasmäe (p.c. 2020); Mariann Bernhardt and Denys Teptiuk (p.c. 2022)
Comment: In the past tense, it is possible to use the copula with predicate nominals, but predicate nominals can also be conjugated in the second past tense.

Name: Can locative constructions commonly occur without a copula?
Comment: In other persons the locative adverbial gets verbal endings: Mon kudo-s-an (1SG house-INE-1SG) 'I am at home'.

Name: Is the same coordinating conjunction commonly used for NPs, VPs, and clauses?
Comment: Coordinating conjunctions are all of Russian origin (Hamari and Ajanki 2022:428). With nouns different types of coordinating is also possible, i.e., Pet'a-t-Mar'a-t (Pet'a-PL-Mar'a-PL) 'Pete and Mary' or Pet'a Mar'a marto (Pet'a Mar'a with) 'Pete with Mary' (Niina Aasmäe, p.c. 2020).

Name: Does existential negation have a special negator?
Comment: In existential sentences, the negation particle aras' is used. In other sentences, the negation particle a is used in the present tense and the negation verb ez' in the past tense.

Name: Can standard negation be asymmetric in the present tense?
Comment: In the present tense, negation is symmetric, e.g. kort-an : a kort-an (speak-PRS.1SG : NEG speak-PRS.1SG) 'I speak : I don't speak'.

Name: Can non-finite dependent clauses use the standard negation construction?
Source: Niina Aasmäe (p.c. 2020); Mariann Bernhardt and Denys Teptiuk (p.c. 2022)

Name: Is there a special negator that combines with non-finite forms?
Source: Niina Aasmäe (p.c. 2020); Mariann Bernhardt and Denys Teptiuk (p.c. 2022)
Comment: Participle and gerund forms are negated with the negative construction apak + connegative, while other constructions are used with finite verbs.

Name: Are there special negative non-finite forms?
Source: Niina Aasmäe (p.c. 2020); Mariann Bernhardt and Denys Teptiuk (p.c. 2022)

Name: Is there an impersonal passive construction?
Source: Niina Aasmäe (p.c. 2020); Mariann Bernhardt and Denys Teptiuk (p.c. 2022)

Name: Are there non-finite forms specializing in the relativization of particular core participants, e.g., subjects (S/A), direct objects (P), absolutive participants (S/P)?
Comment: E.g., the participle in -i(c'a) is A/S-oriented, whereas the participle in -z’ is S/P oriented.

Name: Are there non-finite forms that can relativize all core participants (S/A/P) and possibly also some non-core participants?
Are there non-finite forms that can relativize at least some non-core participants but cannot relativize all core participants?
Source: Niina Aasmäe (p.c. 2020); Mariann Bernhardt and Denys Teptiuk (p.c. 2022)

Name: Is there a specialized negative participial form, which cannot be analyzed as a specific participial marker + negative marker?
Source: Niina Aasmäe (p.c. 2020); Mariann Bernhardt and Denys Teptiuk (p.c. 2022)

Name: Is there a future participle?
Are there participles that can encode their subject with possessive suffixes?
Source: Niina Aasmäe (p.c. 2020); Mariann Bernhardt and Denys Teptiuk (p.c. 2022)

Name: Are there participles that must agree with the nouns they modify in case and number in all contexts?
Are there non-finite forms that can be used both as participles and action nominalizations?
Source: Niina Aasmäe (p.c. 2020); Mariann Bernhardt and Denys Teptiuk (p.c. 2022)

Name: Are there non-finite forms that can be used both as participles and agentive nominalizations?
Comment: The present participle is also used as an agent noun.

Name: Are there non-finite forms that can be used both as participles and converbs?
Is OV the neutral order in affirmative imperative clauses?
Comment: VO is more common, e.g., saj-t' umar' (take-IMP.2SG apple) 'Take an apple'.

Name: Is it possible to place an object between the negative marker and the main verb in imperative constructions?
Comment: This is, however, rare in Erzya.

Name: Is there diminutive (hypocoristic) marking on verbs?
Can modifiers of action nominalizations in agent and patient roles both be encoded like possessors?
Source: Niina Aasmäe (p.c. 2020); Mariann Bernhardt and Denys Teptiuk (p.c. 2022)

Name: Can the modifier of action nominalizations in the patient role be encoded like a direct object?
Source: Niina Aasmäe (p.c. 2020); Mariann Bernhardt and Denys Teptiuk (p.c. 2022)

Name: Do core adjectives agree with the subject noun in number in the predicative position?
Is there a bound superlative degree marker on property words?
Can adpositions have person/number marking?
Are there adpositions that show a tripartite system?
Is there case compounding?
Are there morphological cases only used with pronouns?
Does the numeral modification of a noun have an effect on its case selection?
Is the singular form of the noun also used with numerals greater than 1?
Comment: Both the singular and the plural is possible with numerals bigger than 1.

Name: Is there a dedicated case that expresses indirect object/recipient/goal (different from a local case)?
Is there a dedicated case that expresses a terminative meaning?
Is there a dedicated case that expresses the absence of a referent (abessive)?
Comment: The abessive can be used as a case-ending or in an adnominal function as an adjective. The latter function might be more frequent. Therefore, the abessive is not considered as a case-ending in most of the grammars.

Name: Is there a dedicated case that expresses motion along or by a referent (prolative)?
Is there a locative case that expresses goal?
Is there a locative case that expresses location?
Is there a locative case that expresses source?
Is there case syncretism for goal and location marking?
Is there case syncretism for location and source marking?
Are there two sets of local cases?
Are there three or more sets of local cases?
Is there a dedicated case that expresses the means by which something is carried out (instrumental) and/or accompaniment (comitative)?
Comment: The inessive -so/-se can also be used as instrumental, e.g., salmuks-so (needle-INS) 'by/with a needle'.

Name: Is there a case that expresses the means by which something is carried out (instrumental), which is different from a case that expresses accompaniment (comitative) or other relations?
Is there a dedicated case that expresses the result of a change of state (translative) and/or a state of being (essive)?
Is there a case that expresses a state of being (essive), which is different from a case that expresses the result of a change of state (translative) or other relations?
Is there a dedicated case that expresses equativity?
Comment: The comparative is used to mark the size or the approximate quantity of a referent.

Name: Is there an inflectional nominal marker for expressing benefactive or destinative semantics independent from the dative?
Comment: The dative is used (see UT082).

Name: Is there (in addition to the basic declension) a declension different from a possessive declension?
Comment: Erzya has three declension types: basic declension, definite declension (expresses that the referent of the noun is known to the speaker and the listener) and the possessive declension.

Name: Is it possible to use the singular with the paired body parts/clothing/accessories that accompany them?
Can the marking of the possessor in the attributive construction vary?
Can the possessor in the attributive construction be encoded with the nominative?
Are singular and plural possessee distinguished in the possessive declension?
Comment: However, there are also syncretic forms: singular and plural possession overlap in most cases.

Name: Can a 2Sg possessive suffix be used to mark definiteness?
Source: Niina Aasmäe (p.c. 2020); Mariann Bernhardt and Denys Teptiuk (p.c. 2022)

Name: Can a 3Sg possessive suffix be used to mark definiteness?
Comment: The 3SG possessive suffix can be used instead of the definite declension with some unique nouns, however, this use is restricted.

Name: Is there a human vs. non-human distinction in independent interrogative pronouns?
Comment: Not discussed.

Name: Does 'who' commonly appear as a relative pronoun used for animates other than humans?
Comment: The pronoun kije 'who' is not used, kona 'who, that, which' is used instead. It appears with animates: loman'-es'/buka-s', kona sa-s' t'eze (person-DEF.NOM/ox-DEF.NOM that come-PST1.3SG here) 'The person/ox that came here' as well as with inanimates t'el'e-s', kona juta-s' (winter-DEF.NOM which pass-PST1.3SG) 'the winter that is over'.

Name: Does 'who' commonly appear as a relative pronoun used for inanimates?
Comment: See the comment in UT107.

Name: Can a demonstrative stem be used as a pronoun with a 3Sg human referent?
Are there possessive suffixes, the position of which varies in relation to case/number suffixes?
Comment: The possessive suffixes usually follow case suffixes. In some cases, however, case suffixes and possessive suffixes are fused.

Name: Are case and number marked cumulatively across the paradigm?
Comment: The singular is unmarked, whereas in the plural the plural suffix t : t' follows the noun, and it is followed by the definiteness marker and the case ending.

Name: Are there different words for mother’s brother and father’s brother?
Comment: леля; l'el'a 'uncle' is used for both.

Name: Are there different words for younger and older relatives for relations other than siblings?
Is there an animacy distinction for both concepts: 'new/young' and 'old'?
Comment: There is only distinction for 'old', i.e. tašto 'old (inanimate), sir'e 'old (animate)'.

Name: Is there a distinction for both 'warm' and 'cold' depending on whether reference is to weather or the temperature of objects?
Comment: There is only distinction for 'cold': jakšamo 'cold' is used about (winter) weather, day (cf. also jakšamo-s' (cold-DEF) 'frost, cold weather), whereas kel'me 'cold' is used in a wide range of meanings, e.g. moda-s' kel'me (soil-DEF cold) 'the soil is cold', ked'-et' kel'me (hand-POSS.2SG cold) 'your hand is cold'.

Name: Is there contrastive length for consonants?
Source: Hamari and Ajanki 2022:397; Lehiste et al. 2003:85
Comment: Geminate consonants only occur on morpheme boundary. They may arise when a consonant-initial suffix is attached to a consonant stem, e.g., мaкссa mɑks-sɑ (give-PRS.3SG>3SG) (Hamari and Ajanki 2022:397). As regards consonant duration, there are phonetically short consonants in the language, and there are geminates, but the potential opposition is not part of a quantity system, since this is a morhophonologically conditioned difference (Lehiste et al. 2003:85).

Name: Is there contrastive length for vowels?
Is there a contrast between short and long unstressed vowels?
Comment: The inherent length of vowels in a word, rather than stress affects the relationship between vowel lengths - stressed U may be shorter than unstressed a: nUla 'a piece of cloth'; intrinsically, the high vowels, u, i are shorter than the low a; the length of the stressed i, u may be equal to (or even longer than) that of the unstressed a.

Name: Is there a contrast in the length of geminate consonants?
Comment: No genuine geminates; at morpheme boundary, false geminates may be formed: van-n-oms (see-FRQ-INF).

Name: Are there more than two phonological quantities?
Comment: Quantity has no phonological significance.

Name: Are there extralong syllables?
Is the main foot structure trochaic?
Comment: Mainly disyllabic trochaic feet are realized in speech, e.g. kUdo-sO-nzo (home-INE-POSS.3SG) 'in his/her home'. Trisyllabic feet, e.g. kUdo-so-st (home-INE-POSS.3PL) are also possible. Stress can move across word boundaries: c'Ora-t'-n'E ez'-t' tUje-vE kudO-v (man-PL-NOM.DEF NEG-PST1.3PL leave-PASS.CNG home-LAT) 'The men did/could not leave for home'. Stress is not distinctly expressed.

Name: Is there lexically distinctive word stress?
Comment: Word stress is relatively free and it does not distinguish between meanings.

Name: Is there morphologically conditioned stress?
Comment: The variants of the emphatic particle (clitic) -jak/-kak/-gak 'even, too' (e.g. sOn-gAk 'he, too') tend to have primary or secondary stress.

Name: Is there fixed primary stress?
Source: Hamari and Ajanki 2022:395; Lehiste et al. 2003:86
Comment: The first syllable tends to be stressed if the word is uttered as a dictionary item; in words with more than two syllables, stressed and unstressed syllables alternate: mOdamAr' 'potato' (Niina Aasmäe, p.c. 2020). Stress does not consitute identificational stress but utterance rhythmic structure (Lehiste et al. 2003:86).

Name: Is there stress in heavy syllables?
Is there non-initial primary stress?
Comment: Initial and non-initial stress alternate: mon E-z'in' mO-l'e /mOn e-z'In' mo-l'E (1SG.NOM NEG.PST-1SG go.CNG) 'I did not go'.

Name: Is there palatal vowel harmony?
Is there velar vowel harmony?
Is there labial vowel harmony?
Are there closing diphthongs?
Comment: There are only monophtongs.

Name: Are there opening diphthongs?
Comment: There are only monophtongs.

Name: Is there a qualitative contrast between subsets of short and long vowels?
Are there full vs. reduced vowels as contrastive subsets?
Comment: In South-Eastern dialects, reduced vowels occur in unstressed syllables (showing different patterns of non-contrustive distribution); in some words, a vowel having the quality of a reduced V may appear in a stressed syllable: pUromo-ms/pUrǝmǝ-ms/pƏrǝmǝ-ms 'get together' (Niina Aasmäe, p.c. 2020).

Name: Is there a high front rounded vowel /y/?
Is there a mid-high front rounded vowel /ø/?
Is there a high back unrounded vowel /ɯ ɨ/?
Is there a mid-high back unrounded vowel /ɤ ɘ/?
Is there a low back rounded vowel /ɒ/?
Is there a contrast between mid front vowels (/e/ or /ɛ/) and low front vowels (/æ/ or /a/)?
Comment: In South-Eastern dialects, /e/ vs. /æ/: keď 'hand, arm' vs. käď 'leather, skin'; word-final e, i > ä: śimi > śimä 's/he drinks' .

Name: Are there word-initial consonant clusters?
Are there word-internal consonant clusters in the syllable onset?
Are there consonant clusters in the syllable coda?
Are there word-internal intervocalic clusters of three or more consonants?
Comment: They occur mainly in suffixes (as the result of elision).

Name: Are there word-final clusters of three or more consonants?
Comment: CC(C) occurring mainly in suffixes (as the result of vowel elision).

Name: Are there palatalized alveolar consonants?
Are there palatalized labial and velar consonants?
Comment: Before front vowels, labials and velars may be palatalized.

Name: Is there a palatal stop?
Is there a palatal nasal /ɲ/?
Is there a palatal lateral /ʎ/?
Is there a velar nasal /ŋ/?
Comment: Dialect variation: kov/koŋ 'moon, month', positional nasalization n + k, g: kengeľ-ems 'to tell lies', ponkst 'trousers', kenkš 'a door' (Niina Aasmäe, p.c. 2020).

Name: Is there an alveolar affricate /t͡s/?
Is there a non-alveolar affricate (e.g., /t͡ʃ/, /t͡ɕ/)?
Is there a phonological contrast between an unvoiced alveolar sibilant /s/ and an unvoiced postalveolar sibilant /ʃ/?
Are there voiced plosives (e.g., /b g d/)?
Is there a glottal stop /ʔ/?
Is there a phonological contrast between plain and labial velar consonants (e.g., /k/ vs. /kʷ/)?
Is there a constraint on the word-initial alveolar trill /r/?
Comment: raužo 'black', raśke 'ethnos, people', ruma-ms 'become bleached', Rav 'Volga', ruz 'Russian'.

Name: Is there a laryngeal fricative /h/?
Is there a voiced velar fricative /ɣ/?
Is there a voiceless velar fricative /x/?
Comment: Only in loanwords.

Name: Is there a voiced dental fricative /ð/?
Is there a labio-velar approximant /w/?
Comment: A positional variant: v > w/u, e.g. avol' > awul'/aul' 'not' (Niina Aasmäe, p.c. 2020).

Name: Is there a voiceless labiodental fricative /f/?
Comment: Only in loanwords, e.g., fizika 'physics', Feďa/Xveďa (name) (Niina Aasmäe, p.c. 2020).

Name: Is there radical qualitative consonant gradation?
Is there quantitative consonant gradation?
